subject: Biology
source: Liberia Ministry of Education National Curriculum

topics.topic: Introduction to Biology and Its Branches; The Study of Cell as the Basic Unit of Life; Movement of Substances Across Cell Membrane
objectives: Define biology and discuss some of its branches
Discover major contributors to the development of biology
Compare the characteristics of living things and non-living things
Relate the structures and composition of the cell in relation to their functions
Compare the basic functions of tissues, organs and systems
Demonstrate the use of the microscope in studying Biology
Determine the difference amongst Prokaryotic, Eukaryotic, and Akaryotic cells
Discover the difference between the plant and animal cells
Examine the movement of substances into and out of the cell
Appreciate that all living organisms are made up of cells and that the cell is the building block of life
content: Definition of Biology and Major Branches: Zoology and Botany
Contributors to biology: Aristotle, Linnaeus, Pasteur, Koch, Mendel
Characteristics of Living vs Non-living things: nutrition, respiration, excretion, irritability, movement, growth, reproduction
Euglena: an organism bordering between animals and plants
Biological tool: Light microscopes
Cell and Cell Theory: Basic structures and functions of parts of a cell
Movement of substances into and out of the cell: osmosis, diffusion, facilitated diffusion, active transport, endocytosis (pinocytosis, phagocytosis), and exocytosis
activities: Using concept map, illustrate the branches of biology
Stating the contributions of some scientists to the field of biology
Describing branches of biology related to STIs (Microbiology, Parasitology, Virology, Bacteriology)
Distinguish the basic characteristics of living things including reproduction
Homework: Drawing cells (animal & plant) and labeling their parts
LAB: Draw and label the light Microscope and outline functions of each part
LAB: Identify laboratory materials and apparatus and discuss their uses
LAB: Use microscope to observe onion epidermal cell and cheek cells
primary_texts: Baffour Asante-Owusu, et al. Senior High Biology (Longman, 2009)
supplementary_resources: Sue Hocking, et al. OCR Biology (OCR/Heinemann, 2008)
Doris Koto, et al., Senior Secondary Guide – Biology (Pearson, 2000)
Senior Secondary Guide Biology (Star Study Guide Series) Martin Barker & David Darch 2nd edition, 2016
Bob McDuell, Senior High Integrated Science (Pearson, 2009)

topics.topic: The Hierarchy and Diversity of Living Things; Unicellular Organisms
objectives: Outline the diversity of living things
Discuss the basis of taxonomy (classification)
Discuss the relationship of viruses bordering between living and non-living things
List the major characteristics of the kingdoms Monera, Protista, Fungi, Plantae and Animalia
Classify organisms into kingdom, phylum, class, order, family, genus and species
Explain the basic characteristics of unicellular organisms
Name unicellular organisms that are causative agents of diseases and the diseases they cause
content: Classification and the importance of living things
Classification of organisms into Kingdom, Phylum, Class, Order, Family, Genus and Species
Unicellular organisms and STI-causing agents: Fungus, Bacteria (gonorrhea, syphilis), Virus (HIV/AIDS), Protozoa (Trichomonas Vaginalis)
Sporozoa (plasmodium): causes, effects & preventive methods
Parasitic protozoa and diseases: Entamoeba histolytica (Amebic dysentery), Giardia lamblia (giardiasis), Plasmodium falciparum (malaria)
activities: List the general characteristics of each kingdom
LAB: Draw and label one organism from each of the five kingdoms
Draw and label structures of unicellular organisms: Amoeba, Paramecium, Euglena
Observing unicellular organisms under a microscope from brackish/pond water
Draw the life cycle of the plasmodium
List and discuss causative agents of STIs and diseases they cause
Discussion of effects and preventions of malaria and dysentery
primary_texts: Baffour Asante-Owusu, et al. Senior High Biology (Longman, 2009)
supplementary_resources: Sue Hocking, et al. OCR Biology (OCR/Heinemann, 2008)
Doris Koto, et al., Senior Secondary Guide – Biology (Pearson, 2000)
Senior Secondary Guide Biology (Star Study Guide Series) Martin Barker & David Darch 2nd edition, 2016
Bob McDuell, Senior High Integrated Science (Pearson, 2009)

objectives: Discuss the functions of the four types of tissues
Explain the concept of organ as a combination of tissues and systems as a combination of organs
Describe the general characteristics of multicellular organisms
Describe the general characteristics and morphological features of sponges and hydra
Classify and structurally differentiate worms
Explain parasitism among worms and the alternative hosts considering their life cycles
Outline measures for preventing parasitic worm infections
Differentiate between the leech and earth worm based on morphology
Compare the structures and functions of the human reproductive systems
Identify the various stages of the menstrual cycle and explain essence of contraceptive
Recognize that substance abuse (alcohol and drugs) is harmful to life
content: Tissues, Organs, and Systems
General characteristics of Sponges and Hydra
Worms: flat worms (Planarian, blood & liver flukes, tape worms), parasitic round worms (ascaris, hook worm, filarial worm, trichina worm), segmented worms (earth worm and leeches)
Human reproductive structures: male and female reproductive organs and functions
Human Life cycle: infancy, juvenile, adolescence, adult, senescence
Menstruation, menstrual hygiene, pregnancy and STIs prevention
Abstinence, use of condoms, contraceptives
Substance abuse and sexual desires: definition, names, classification, effects, prevention
activities: Explanation of tissues in relation to organs and systems
Draw and label the body structure of a sponge with functions
Draw and label the parts of a hydra with functions
Explanation of conditions for oral transmission of intestinal parasites
Outlining effects, symptoms and methods of prevention of intestinal parasites
Dissecting an earth worm and identifying external and internal features
Observe and draw external structures of filarial worm, tape worm, hook worm, round worm
Use visual aids to demonstrate natural family planning method
Drama: A female refusing to have sex because of unsafe period
Case study on influences of sexual desires
Role play on resisting things that influence sexual desires
primary_texts: Baffour Asante-Owusu, et al. Senior High Biology (Longman, 2009)
supplementary_resources: Sue Hocking, et al. OCR Biology (OCR/Heinemann, 2008)
Doris Koto, et al., Senior Secondary Guide – Biology (Pearson, 2000)
Senior Secondary Guide Biology (Star Study Guide Series) Martin Barker & David Darch 2nd edition, 2016
Bob McDuell, Senior High Integrated Science (Pearson, 2009)
lab_materials: Charts on various types of tissues, organs, and systems
Charts/specimens of various kinds of worms

topics.topic: Arthropods and Biological Control of Pests
objectives: Describe and classify arthropods according to their morphology
Explain the process of metamorphosis (complete & incomplete) and Ecdysis (molting) in arthropods
Discuss the role of vectors (cockroach, mosquito, house-fly, and tsetse fly)
Explain the general characteristics of butterfly
Elaborate on the economic importance of honey bees and termites
Discuss pests, their economic importance and control measures
Describe the features and economic importance of grasshoppers
content: Arthropod: General characteristics & classification
Morphology and life processes: respiration, feeding, mouth parts and body segments
Life cycle: metamorphosis and molting (complete and incomplete)
Vectors: Mosquitoes, tsetse fly, house fly, cockroach — mouth parts, feeding, life cycle, disease transmission
Caste System in Social Insects
Economic importance of social insects: honey bees and termites
Pests: economic importance, chemical control, biological control
activities: Field trip: collection of different species of insects outdoor
Study specimen: grasshopper/locust or cockroach, weevils and cotton stainers
Discussing the economic importance of arthropods
Diagramming the life cycle of mosquitoes (anopheles) in relationship to the plasmodium (malaria)
Assignment: Collecting mosquito larvae/wigglers for lab observation
Listing methods of controlling the spread of malaria
Draw and label parts of each class of arthropods
Stating the economic importance of honey bees and termites
Discussing honey bees and termites as social insects
primary_texts: Baffour Asante-Owusu, et al. Senior High Biology (Longman, 2009)
supplementary_resources: Sue Hocking, et al. OCR Biology (OCR/Heinemann, 2008)
Doris Koto, et al., Senior Secondary Guide – Biology (Pearson, 2000)
Senior Secondary Guide Biology (Star Study Guide Series) Martin Barker & David Darch 2nd edition, 2016
Bob McDuell, Senior High Integrated Science (Pearson, 2009)
lab_materials: Charts on various kinds of arthropods and malaria cycle
Specimens: crab, crayfish, spiders, centipede, millipede, grasshoppers, butterflies, cockroaches, weevils and cotton stainers

topics.topic: Plant-Like Organisms (Algae, Mosses, Ferns), Photosynthesis, and Fungi
objectives: Describe the general characteristics, structures and life cycles of algae, mosses, ferns, and Fungi
Explain the economic importance of algae and fungi to humans
Describe the process of reproduction in algae
Explain types of nutrition of fungi
Describe symbiotic relationship of Fungi in relation to parasitism and saprophytism
List common fungal diseases of plants and animals
Explain the process of photosynthesis
content: Algae: general characteristics, classification, phytoplankton, green algae, Spirogyra reproduction (sexual and asexual), economic importance
Mosses (Brachymerium and Funaria): general characteristics, reproduction (alternation of generations), economic importance
Ferns (Nephrolepis, Platycerium): general characteristics, reproduction, economic importance
Fungi: general characteristics, classification, nutrition, parasitic and saprophytic modes of life
Fungal diseases: blight, smuts, rust, athlete's foot, yeast infection, ringworm, eczema
Fungi reproduction (sexual & asexual) and economic importance
Photosynthesis: definition, conditions, leaf adaptation, light dependent and independent reactions, products, macronutrients and micronutrients
activities: Drawing and labeling parts of a spirogyra
Drawing and labeling stages of sexual reproduction in spirogyra
Observing and identifying molded bread under microscope, drawing parts of rhizopus hyphae
Illustrating the life cycle of rhizopus
Explaining life cycle of a club fungus
Collecting and studying a bracket fungus
Stating ways of preventing fungal infections
Drawing and labeling life cycles of mosses and ferns
Experimenting: growing plants in sunlight vs shade
Wrapping leaves with aluminum foil to compare photosynthesis effects
Testing a leaf for starch
Testing to extract chlorophyll
Experimenting to demonstrate need for chlorophyll in photosynthesis
primary_texts: Baffour Asante-Owusu, et al. Senior High Biology (Longman, 2009)
supplementary_resources: Sue Hocking, et al. OCR Biology (OCR/Heinemann, 2008)
Doris Koto, et al., Senior Secondary Guide – Biology (Pearson, 2000)
Senior Secondary Guide Biology (Star Study Guide Series) Martin Barker & David Darch 2nd edition, 2016
Bob McDuell, Senior High Integrated Science (Pearson, 2009)
lab_materials: Charts on algae, mosses, ferns & fungi
Specimens (yeast, molded bread, club fungi, bracket fungi)
Plain slides & prepared slides, cover slips

objectives: Identify the characteristics of flowering plants and distinguish them
Classify flowering plants into monocotyledonae (monocots) and dicotyledonae (dicots)
Distinguish the structural characteristics of monocots and dicots
Describe the structures and functions of roots, stems, leaves, and flowers
Explain sexual and asexual reproduction in flowering plants
Determine floral formulae of flowers
Discuss types of pollination and list agents of pollination
Explain the process of zygote and embryo formation
Describe conditions necessary for seed germination
List the types of fruits and explain fruit and seed dispersal
Describe plant hormones and their functions
Explain transport system in plants
Discuss excretion in plants
Describe plant growth and development
Explain gaseous exchange in plants
content: Flowering plants classification: monocots & dicots
Functions of roots, stems, leaves and flowers
Floral formulae: Flamboyant (Delonix), Pride of Barbados (Caesalpinia), Rattle Box (Crotalaria)
Types of plant tissues
Root system: types, regions of root tip, functions and structures of root hairs
Modified roots, stems and leaves: tubers, bulbs, tendrils, runners
Leaf classification and arrangement on stem
Germination: epigeal and hypogeal types, conditions for germination
Reproduction in flowering plants
Kinds of fruits and dispersal agents
Plant Hormones: primary and secondary growth, Nastic and Tactic movements
Transport system in vascular plants: water and minerals, organic materials
Excretion in plants: water, CO2, oxygen, alkaloids, tannins, resins, acids, gums
Pressure flow hypothesis and cytoplasmic streaming
Transpiration: advantages and disadvantages
Gaseous exchange: concentration gradient, stomata, lenticels
Metabolic equations for respiration
Types of respiration: facultative aerobic and anaerobic
activities: Drawing and labeling parts of a complete flower with functions
Illustration of types of vegetative propagation (cutting, grafting)
LAB: Setting up experiment to demonstrate germination types using corn and bean seeds
Examine internal structure of leaf using microscope
Collecting and classifying various fruits and seeds
Drawing and labeling cross section of monocot and dicot stems and roots
Explaining types of pollination and listing agents
Observing transpiration through experiments
Field Trip: Collecting and classifying different kinds of leaves
Examining sections of stems and roots showing growth stages
primary_texts: Baffour Asante-Owusu, et al. Senior High Biology (Longman, 2009)
supplementary_resources: Sue Hocking, et al. OCR Biology (OCR/Heinemann, 2008)
Doris Koto, et al., Senior Secondary Guide – Biology (Pearson, 2000)
Senior Secondary Guide Biology (Star Study Guide Series) Martin Barker & David Darch 2nd edition, 2016
Bob McDuell, Senior High Integrated Science (Pearson, 2009)
lab_materials: Specimens of whole plant with roots, stem, leaves & flowers

objectives: List the characteristics of viruses
Classify viruses based on nucleic acid (DNA & RNA)
Explain the life cycle of a virus
List some viral diseases and organisms they attack, modes of transmission and methods of prevention
Describe bacteria of various kinds
Classify bacteria
List and describe some common bacterial diseases and symptoms
Outline preventive measures of bacterial diseases
Distinguish between autotrophic and heterotrophic nutrition; aerobic, anaerobic and facultative respiration
Explain the economic importance of bacteria
content: Virus: definition, general characteristics, composition of viral structure
Classification: DNA viruses and RNA viruses
Common viral diseases: cold, flu, mumps, chicken pox, rabies, polio, HIV/AIDS
Life cycle of a virus: Lytic Cycle and Lysogenic Cycle
STIs: modes of transmission and prevention
Structure of bacteriophage
Bacteria: definition, general characteristics, classification and shape, composition structure
Common bacterial diseases: tuberculosis, tetanus, streptococcus
STIs caused by bacteria: modes of transmission and prevention
activities: Listing and discussing viruses and bacteria that cause diseases
Identifying and listing common viral and bacterial diseases
Discussing STIs caused by viruses and bacteria
Mode of transmission and prevention
Discussing the importance of HIV testing and support
Diagramming the life cycle of bacteriophage
Group discussion on causes and preventive measures in controlling STIs
primary_texts: Baffour Asante-Owusu, et al. Senior High Biology (Longman, 2009)
supplementary_resources: Sue Hocking, et al. OCR Biology (OCR/Heinemann, 2008)

objectives: Explain the process of nutrition and state why living things need nutrients
Outline and classify the types of nutrients found in food
Classify food into groups
Demonstrate the presence of various nutrients found in food
Explain the concept of a balanced diet
Explain the concept of malnutrition
Determine the dental formula of a mammal
Explain the importance of dental care in humans
Name and discuss various methods of preserving and storing food
Explain methods of preserving food using local resources
Explain other methods of food preservation in West Africa
Explain the biological basis for preserving and storing food
content: Nutrition: definition and types (autotrophic, heterotrophic, holozoic)
Food and nutrients: carbohydrates, lipids, proteins, vitamins, mineral salts and water
Balanced diet
Malnutrition
Teeth and dental formulae
Dental care
Food Poisoning and its Prevention
Methods of food preservation: drying, salting, smoking, parboiling, dehydration, refrigeration, frying, use of oil, heating
activities: Classifying the nutrients found in different types of food
LAB: Testing for carbohydrate — reducing and non-reducing sugar (Benedict's test), starch (iodine test)
LAB: Testing for lipid (emulsion test)
LAB: Testing for proteins (biuret test)
LAB: Testing for vitamin C
Using preservative methods on food samples and comparing with unpreserved food

Senior Secondary Guide Biology (Star Study Guide Series) Martin Barker & David Darch 2nd edition, 2016

topics.topic: Soil, Energy and Ecology — Patterns in Nature
objectives: Define soil and state the composition of soil
State the effects of erosion and overuse of soil on soil fertility
Explain the processes of soil conservation, maintenance, and renewal of soil fertility
Explain advantages and disadvantages of slash and burn methods in farming
Distinguish between habitat and niche
Describe the concept of ecological succession
Define and calculate population growth, doubling time, percentage growth rate, death & birth rates
Describe inter-specific and intra-specific interactions among organisms
Discuss the ecosystem (food chains, food webs, pyramids of numbers, pyramid of energy)
Define productivity of an ecosystem and distinguish gross primary and net primary productivity
Discuss energy flow through trophic levels and biogeochemical cycles
Distinguish between immigration and emigration
content: Soil: formation, composition, types, fertility, erosion (causes and prevention), conservation, maintenance, renewal
Weathering: physical and chemical
Liberia food and cash crops production
Effects of non-biodegradable substances on soil fertility
Isolation mechanisms of species
Inter-specific interactions: mutualism, commensalism, predation, parasitism, competition
Trophic levels: producers, consumers, decomposers
Food chains and webs
Conservation of nature: soil, forest, wildlife, oil, mineral conservation
Biocycles: water cycle, carbon cycle, nitrogen cycle, phosphorus cycle, sulfur cycle
Organisms habitat and niche
Population: density, growth rate, doubling time, birth rate, death rate, immigration, emigration
Ecological succession: primary and secondary, pioneer and climax communities
activities: Explaining soil formation
LAB: Collecting, observing and classifying soil types
Listing and discussing the composition of soil
Demonstrating the presence of air in the soil (moisture content)
Diagramming food chains and food webs
Diagramming and discussing water, carbon, nitrogen, phosphorus and sulfur cycles
Field Trip: Observing effects of erosion on soil fertility
Digging in school yard/dump sites to observe non-biodegradable substances
Listing food and cash crops in Liberia
Discussing various inter-specific interactions between species
Taking field trips to visit ecosystems such as ponds and forest regions

supplementary_resources: Sue Hocking, et al. OCR Biology (OCR/Heinemann, 2008)

topics.topic: Cell Growth and Division (Mitosis and Meiosis); Reproduction
objectives: Describe the stages of the cell cycle
List and diagram the phases of mitosis and meiosis
Distinguish mitosis and meiosis and explain the importance of meiosis in sexual reproduction
Distinguish between asexual and sexual reproduction
List and explain some forms of asexual reproduction in plants and animals
Discuss reproduction and parenting in humans (sexuality)
Recognize sexual decisions that have impact on the family
Initiate advocacy on substance abuse and SBV
content: Cell growth & Division: Cell cycle, Phases of Mitosis, Meiosis
Reproduction types: Asexual (fission, budding, vegetative propagation, cloning) and Sexual (conjugation, gamete formation, fertilization)
Responsibilities of parenting: roles of each parent in child rearing
Risk of teenage parenting
Sexual Decisions and Impact on the Family
Reproductive health and rights
Infertility and cycles of sexuality
Consequences of sexual decision making
Advocacy: role of youth in stopping substance abuse and SBV
activities: Drawing and labeling stages of mitosis and meiosis
Distinguishing mitosis and meiosis
Explaining gametes formation and terms (gametes, diploid, haploid)
LAB: Examining thin slices of onion root tip to study stages of mitosis under microscope
Individual writing: What kind of family you intend to have in the next ten years
Personal Experience sharing: Invite a respected father to talk about parenting
Role plays: refusing to have sex, discouraging peers from risky behavior
Steps in the correct use of condoms
Discussion on sexual decisions and impact on individual and family
Skit on negative and positive decision making about sex
Draw on posters, write poems, compose songs against drug abuse and School Based Violence

topics.topic: Genetics (Nucleic Acids, Protein Synthesis, and Heredity), Sexuality and Evolution
objectives: Explain the term nucleic acids and name the types
Describe the double helix model of DNA structure
Outline the process of DNA replication and RNA transcription
Explain the process of protein synthesis
Explain the meaning of genetics, heredity, and sexuality
Describe how traits are passed from parents to offspring
Explain Mendel's contributions to the principles of heredity
Demonstrate genetic principles on Mendel's experiment with garden peas
Discuss linkage and sex-linked characters
Discuss Darwin's and Lamarck's theories of evolution
List the various types of blood groups and state the type of blood needed for transfusion
content: Nucleic acids: DNA and RNA structures, types of RNA
Structures of nucleotides and complementary base pairing
DNA replication and RNA transcription
Stages of protein synthesis and its importance
Genetics and Heredity: Mendel's experiment with garden peas
Genetic terms: phenotype, genotype, alleles, hybrid, homozygous, heterozygous, monohybrid, dihybrid
Hereditary traits: hemophilia, mental disorder, sickle cell, color blindness, baldness, ear lobes
Influence of environment on heredity
ABO blood grouping and rhesus factor
Evolution and natural selection (Darwin's Theory)
Sexuality: sex determination (X and Y chromosomes)
Variation: continuous and discontinuous
Sources of variation: crossing over, independent assortment, random fusion of gametes
Population genetics
Convergent and Divergent evolution
Evidence of evolution: fossil records
Theories of evolution: Lamarck's theory and Darwin's theory
activities: Using DNA model to demonstrate DNA replication
Using charts to explain RNA transcription
Using charts to demonstrate protein synthesis
Describing Mendel's contributions to principles of heredity
Describing Mendel's experiments and results
Solving monohybrid and dihybrid problems using Punnett square
Discussing some genetic disorders and diseases
Outlining similarity and differences among different species of vertebrates

topics.topic: Excretory and Respiratory Systems; Cellular Respiration (Glycolysis and Krebs Cycle)
objectives: Describe the excretory system and state the functions of all associated organs
List the tissues and organs involved in the mechanism of breathing
Explain homeostasis in relation to the excretory system
Explain the effects of substance abuse and STIs on excretory and respiratory systems
State the characteristics of types of respiration
Distinguish between aerobic and anaerobic respiration
Discuss cellular respiration: Glycolysis, Krebs cycle, and electron transport chain
Discuss anaerobic respiration in muscle and fermentation using yeast/fruits
Discuss the significance of phosphorylation in glycolysis
Identify final products of glycolysis and fate of pyruvate
Distinguish oxidation and reduction with regards to oxygen, hydrogen and electrons
Distinguish decarboxylation and dehydrogenation reactions
Interpret the balanced chemical equation for respiration
Identify electron carriers in the inner membrane of mitochondria
content: Excretory system organs: kidneys, urinary bladder, urethra, skin, liver, lungs, large intestine
Respiratory system organs: lungs, pharynx, larynx, alveoli, bronchi, bronchioles
Effects of substance abuse and STIs on both systems
Respiration (Gaseous Exchange): internal & external, phases (inspiration and expiration)
Cellular Respiration: aerobic and anaerobic, energy release
ATP formation as a phosphorylated nucleotide
Overview of respiration: glycolysis, link reaction, Krebs cycle, electron transport chain
Coenzymes and respiration: NAD and dehydrogenase enzymes
Pyruvate and its fate
Alcoholic fermentation (yeast and fruits)
Anaerobic respiration in muscles and Oxygen debt
Reactions of Krebs cycle: decarboxylation, dehydrogenation, oxidative phosphorylation
Electron transport chain: flavoproteins, quinones, cytochromes
activities: Explaining the process of excretion and urination
Describing functions of tissues and organs in external & internal respiration
Describing the lungs and air passageways
LAB: Drawing and labeling longitudinal section of the kidney
Class Discussion: role of diaphragm, intercostal muscles and ribs in respiration
Video/pictures showing organs affected by substance abuse and STIs
Vigorous exercise exemplifying respiration
LAB: Obtaining palm wine and placing in plastic gallon to observe alcoholic fermentation
Demonstrate artificial resuscitation

supplementary_resources: Sue Hocking, et al. OCR Biology (OCR/Heinemann, 2008)

lab_materials: Charts/posters on kidneys, lungs, skin, and urinary organs

topics.topic: Nervous and Endocrine Systems (Control and Co-ordination of Body Activities)
objectives: Distinguish the functions of the nervous and endocrine systems
Describe the structure and functions of a nerve cell (neuron) and the brain
Classify the neurons of the nervous system
Draw the nervous system and list the major parts
Describe the structure and functions of the spinal cord
Differentiate the various regions of the spinal cord
Compare central and peripheral nervous systems
Differentiate between voluntary and involuntary actions
Discuss causes and effects of substance abuse on the nervous system
Advocate for prevention of GBV, rape, sexual harassment, and intergenerational sex
Explain the effects of some STIs on the nervous system
Describe the structures and functions of the eye and ear
Distinguish and state functions of exocrine and endocrine glands
Explain the regulation of hormone secretion through negative feedback
Describe the two basic mechanisms of hormone action
content: Nervous system: central nervous system and peripheral nervous system
Spinal cord: neurons structure and types, sensory and motor functions
Brain: structure and function of parts
Generation and transmission of nerve impulses: resting potential, action potential, refractory period, myelin sheath, synapses
Types of Nervous actions: voluntary, involuntary, reflex and reflex arc
Autonomic nervous system: functions and importance
Structure & function of eye and ear
GBV, rape, sexual harassment and intergenerational sex
Effects of STIs on organs of the nervous system
Substance abuse: causes, effects and prevention
Endocrine system: glands and hormones
Role of other organs as endocrine glands: testes, ovaries, liver, kidneys, stomach
Hormone deficiency diseases
activities: Listing and describing parts of the nervous system
Examining and explaining models of the brain and spinal cord
Identifying various parts of the brain and spinal cord by drawing and labeling
Describing the peripheral nervous system
Describing structures and functions of the eye and ear
Explaining nervous actions
Listing organs affected by STIs and substance abuse
CONTINUUM: Drawing posters, writing poems, composing songs against drug abuse, GBV and intergenerational sex
Organizing a hot line for victims
Explaining causes and corrections of eye defects
Drawing and labeling the skin as a sense organ
Drawing and labeling a typical motor neuron
Examining model and chart of mammalian eye and ear

Model of brain, spinal cord, eye and ear

topics.topic: Ecology (Natural Resources and Pollution) and Health
objectives: Explain the concept of natural resources
Discuss the importance of natural resources
Distinguish between renewable and non-renewable natural resources
Explain methods of conserving natural resources
Explain preserving the ecosystem as an approach to natural resource management
Explain the term pollution and discuss causes, effects and control methods
Explain the importance of immunization
Explain the importance of personal health and community health
State the dangers posed by drugs, alcoholic beverages and smoking
Define sewage disposal and discuss methods
Identify economic uses of sewage
Discuss sources of water, modes of contamination and methods of purification
Discuss methods of refuse collection and disposal
State the importance of first aid
content: Natural resources: renewable and non-renewable
Flow of renewable resources
Conservation of natural resources
Pollution: air, water, land, thermal, noise — causes, effects, and control
Vaccination and immunization
Personal hygiene
Drug abuse
Community hygiene
Sewage and sewage disposal: definitions, methods, economic uses
Water: sources, contamination/pollution, purification methods
Refuse collection and disposal
activities: Group work on the importance of conservation of natural resources
Field trips: viewing sites of natural resources (rain forests, mines, rivers, ocean, rubber factory, petroleum refinery)
Field trips: observing solar radiation, tides, winds
Field trips to water sewage treatment plant
Discussing different methods of sewage disposal and uses of sewage
LAB: Purifying water by boiling, chlorination and sand filtration
Testing water for contaminants
Filtering contaminated water using clean cloth
Practicing first aid exercises on partners
Observing nitrogen-fixing bacteria under microscope
Estimating the alcohol content of various drinks
